claim: Data agents require iterative reasoning and decision points throughout execution, unlike coding agents which can execute from a fixed plan upfront
actionable: When building data agents, design workflows that support users saying 'interesting, look at it this way' at multiple decision points; avoid one-shot question-answer patterns
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Inject conflicting context into agents causes cognitive collapse: models spend 30+ minutes pondering when given contradictory information instead of quickly resolving conflicts
actionable: When combining multiple context sources (user memory, team guides, semantic models), validate for conflicts and prioritize or reconcile before injection; watch for 'hmm actually' loops
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: With ~100K tokens of tools available, implement tool search/retrieval to prevent hallucination and keep agent focused on relevant tools
actionable: Add tool search layer that retrieves N most relevant tools based on query; reduces tool descriptions passed to model from 100K tokens to manageable subset
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Ephemeral SQL queries (invisible to users) help agents validate data schema and format before writing final notebook queries, improving UX by reducing refinement loops
actionable: Allow agents to run quick internal SQL probes to check table existence, column formats, join paths; only write visible queries once confident; requires careful prompt guidance
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Most public eval sets for data tasks are poor quality: conflate SQL syntax with analytical reasoning, focus on needle-in-haystack retrieval instead of real decision-making
actionable: When building evals, craft small curated sets (30-50) of hard scenarios that test actual domain reasoning, not SQL parsing; verify each case by hand
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Long-horizon evals (multi-day simulations) are essential for data agents; one-shot evals miss the compounding value of agents improving over time as they learn context
actionable: Design 90-day simulations where database state changes daily, agents receive tickets, update wikis, and improve; measure day-90 performance not day-0 to reflect real-world value
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Hand-crafted, artisanal eval sets focusing on specific failure traps are more useful than large generic benchmarks; keep them small enough to reason about in your head
actionable: Design 30-50 eval questions targeting known failure modes (e.g., 'agent misses data quality bug masked by visualization'); document why each trap matters
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Separate static context (data team guides, semantic models) from dynamic user-level memory to prevent conflicts; user-level memory can contradict admin guidance
actionable: Organize context into tiers: semantic models + admin guides (static, slow-moving), user feedback (dynamic); inject carefully to avoid conflicting signals
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Building custom orchestration (Temporal-based) provided control needed to migrate from single-shot to long-running agents; off-the-shelf tools couldn't iterate fast enough
actionable: If shipping production data agents, evaluate custom orchestration vs frameworks based on: iteration speed needed, control over harness, maintenance overhead tolerance
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Consolidating duplicated tools (e.g., create_chart, update_chart, delete_chart) into unified tools reduces token overhead; redundancy across tools is often unnecessary
actionable: Audit tool specs for repetition; test via evals whether one 'chart tool' works as well as five variants; consolidate if evals pass
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves 24% accuracy on Hex's 90-day simulation after 90 days (up from 4% day-0); shows agents struggle with long-horizon data reasoning even with improvement
actionable: When evaluating data agents, expect realistic models to achieve low accuracy on hard simulations even over time; use this baseline to set expectations
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts

claim: Effort levels in modern models (Claude effort, OpenAI juice) trade latency for accuracy; requires empirical testing to find sweet spot that doesn't force users to wait 10+ minutes
actionable: Test effort/juice settings via evals to find minimum setting that achieves acceptable accuracy; avoid maximum effort unless latency is unconstrained
source_title: How Hex builds AI agents that reason like human data analysts
